sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Intraosseous access provides rapid vascular access when IV access is delayed, difficult, or inappropriate for the urgency of the situation. In critically unwell patients it should be considered early rather than after repeated unsuccessful IV attempts.

sections.title: Sites
sections.content:
- Choose the insertion site according to age, anatomy, access, and device instructions
- Common sites include proximal tibia, proximal humerus in adults, and selected paediatric alternatives within protocol
- Select a needle length that will penetrate soft tissue and enter the medullary cavity confidently

sections.title: Procedure (EZ-IO)
sections.content:
1. Identify the site, prepare the skin, and position the limb appropriately

2. Insert the IO device according to manufacturer instructions using the correct needle size for the patient and site

3. Remove the stylet, attach primed extension tubing, and confirm placement by stability, easy flush, and lack of obvious extravasation

4. Secure the line well and reassess it during movement and transport

5. In conscious patients, treat infusion pain according to protocol before large flushes or infusions where appropriate

6. Give medications and fluids through the IO with appropriate flushes and pressure assistance when needed

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content:
- IO access can take most emergency IV drugs and fluids using the same doses unless a specific drug guidance says otherwise
- Fluids often need pressure to run effectively through an IO line
- Avoid sites with fracture, major local infection, or recent previous IO access according to protocol
- Document the site, device, confirmation, patient tolerance, and response
